content.text: Network requests fail. Servers return errors. Rate limits are hit. Robust API clients treat failures as expected events and handle them gracefully through structured error classification, standardised error payloads, and retry strategies that respect the server's capacity constraints.
API error handling encompasses two complementary disciplines:
(returning errors that communicate exactly what went wrong) and
(interpreting errors and deciding whether to retry, surface to the user, or fail fast). Both sides must cooperate to produce a robust integration.

On the server side, error handling means mapping every failure mode to the correct HTTP status code, returning a consistent error body schema, and including enough context for the client to distinguish recoverable from non-recoverable errors. On the client side, it means parsing error responses, classifying errors by retryability, implementing exponential backoff with jitter, and surfacing meaningful exceptions to the application layer.

in the body is untestable, unmonitorable, and forces every client to parse the body to detect failures. CDN caches may cache the 200 response. Retry logic cannot act on it without body inspection. Observability tools cannot distinguish successes from failures.
Conversely, a client that catches every exception with

will silently drop failures, confusing debugging and causing data integrity problems. A client that retries every error immediately and without backoff will amplify a transient overload into a cascade failure.

For agentic systems calling external APIs as tools, error handling determines whether a failed tool call causes the agent to crash, retry sensibly, or fall back to a degraded strategy. An agent with good error handling can survive transient failures; one without it fails on the first network hiccup.
Think of API errors as a
your request is wrong — fix it before trying again

. Running a red light (retrying a 400) wastes resources and will never succeed. Ignoring yellow and accelerating (retrying immediately without backoff) causes collisions (cascade failures). Respecting the signals produces smooth, efficient flow.
— retrying 5xx errors and network failures recovers automatically from brief outages without user intervention.
— exponential backoff reduces load on an already-struggling server, giving it time to recover rather than amplifying the overload.
— not retrying 4xx errors gives immediate feedback to the application layer rather than wasting time on doomed retries.
— structured error responses with error codes and messages enable client-side logging and alerting that goes beyond "something failed".
— combining retries with idempotency keys ensures that safe retries of POST requests do not create duplicate side effects.
— retrying aggressively during an outage multiplies the load on the server. Exponential backoff with jitter and a maximum retry count mitigates this.
— a request that requires two retries takes three times as long. Set maximum retry budgets appropriate to the caller's timeout.
— retrying non-idempotent operations (creating records, charging cards) without idempotency keys causes duplicate side effects.
— network timeouts are ambiguous: the server may have completed the operation before the connection dropped. Retrying a timed-out POST without an idempotency key may duplicate work.

Status codes are the primary error classification signal. The 4xx range indicates client errors (the client must fix the request); the 5xx range indicates server errors (the client may retry). Within these ranges, specific codes communicate specific failure modes:
— malformed syntax or failed validation. Do not retry.
— missing or invalid authentication. Refresh credentials and retry once.
— authenticated but not authorised. Do not retry.
— resource does not exist. Do not retry.
— state conflict (e.g., duplicate). Do not retry unless the conflict is transient.
— valid syntax but semantic errors. Do not retry.
— upstream proxy failure. Retry with backoff.
— server overloaded or in maintenance. Retry after
— upstream timed out. Retry with backoff.

Exponential backoff spaces retries geometrically: after the first failure wait 1 second, after the second 2 seconds, after the third 4 seconds, and so on, up to a cap. Jitter adds a random fraction of the delay to prevent
— multiple clients that all start retrying in sync, peaking the load every backoff interval.

attrs.alt: API error handling with exponential backoff retry flow

content.text: The diagram shows a client that retries a 503 error twice with increasing waits before succeeding on the third attempt. The decision table on the right classifies each error type by retryability. Jitter prevents all clients from retrying at the same instant.
Any HTTP client that calls a remote API — retry logic and structured error handling should be the default, not an afterthought.
Agentic tool calls — agents must distinguish recoverable tool errors (retry) from invalid tool arguments (report error to the LLM) from permanent failures (abort).
Payment and order APIs — idempotency keys are essential when retrying POST requests that have financial side effects.
LLM provider APIs — providers return 429 during high load; retry with backoff is necessary for production pipelines.

# FastAPI server-side error responses using RFC 9457 Problem Details
from fastapi import FastAPI, HTTPException, Request
from fastapi.responses import JSONResponse
from pydantic import BaseModel, ValidationError

app = FastAPI()

class ProblemDetail(BaseModel):
    type: str = "about:blank"
    title: str
    status: int
    detail: str
    instance: str | None = None

@app.exception_handler(HTTPException)
async def http_exception_handler(request: Request, exc: HTTPException):
    problem = ProblemDetail(
        title=exc.detail,
        status=exc.status_code,
        detail=exc.detail,
        instance=str(request.url),
    )
    return JSONResponse(
        status_code=exc.status_code,
        content=problem.model_dump(exclude_none=True),
        headers={"Content-Type": "application/problem+json"},
    )

@app.exception_handler(ValidationError)
async def validation_exception_handler(request: Request, exc: ValidationError):
    return JSONResponse(
        status_code=422,
        content={
            "type": "https://example.com/errors/validation",
            "title": "Validation Error",
            "status": 422,
            "detail": "One or more fields failed validation.",
            "errors": exc.errors(),
        },
        headers={"Content-Type": "application/problem+json"},
    )
Handling errors in an agent tool call
errors during high load. Production pipelines that call the API in agentic loops must handle these gracefully. The recommended approach is to check the
header after each response and proactively slow down when the remaining quota falls below a threshold, rather than waiting for a 429.

An ML pipeline at a data company processed 500,000 documents per day through an LLM API. They implemented a token-bucket rate limiter in front of the API client, keeping request rate 20% below the API's limit. When 429s did occur (during quota resets), the client respected

exactly. Retry logic used exponential backoff with jitter, capped at 60 seconds and 5 attempts. The overall pipeline success rate was 99.95%, with 0.05% of requests failing after exhausting retries and being queued for reprocessing.

Retrying a 400, 401, 403, 404, or 422 is always wrong — the client sent a bad request, and the server will return the same error regardless of how many times it is retried. This wastes quota and delays the eventual failure.

A fixed delay (e.g., always wait 1 second) means all clients that received errors at the same time retry simultaneously, creating a burst that may again exceed capacity. Jitter spreads retries over time, smoothing the load.
Logging only the HTTP status code and not the error body loses the server's detailed explanation. An HTTP 400 with
{"detail": "email field must be a valid email address"}
Parse the error body, log with full context, raise a typed exception with the server's error message
on the server for a standardised error envelope that clients can parse generically.
alongside human-readable messages, so clients can programmatically distinguish error types without parsing strings.
— base * 2^attempt + random(0, base) is the standard formula; cap at 30-60 seconds maximum.
— when the server specifies a wait duration, respect it exactly rather than using the backoff formula.
— infinite retries can exhaust thread pools or block agents indefinitely. Three to five retries is usually sufficient for transient errors.
— include the attempt number, delay, and error so debugging failed pipelines is straightforward.
Use idempotency keys on retried POSTs
— generate a UUID for each logical operation and send it as
Retry strategies directly affect tail latency. A p99 request that requires two retries has a latency of roughly
. Setting timeouts too generously means each failed attempt takes the full timeout duration, multiplying the total wait.
to set separate connect, read, write, and pool timeouts. A typical production configuration: connect=5s, read=30s, write=5s. This prevents a slow server from holding connections open indefinitely.

Under sustained rate limiting, a circuit breaker pattern can prevent the client from making requests that will immediately be rate-limited. After a configured number of consecutive 429s, the circuit opens and all calls fail immediately (with a cached or default response) for a cool-off period.
The standard Python retry library. Supports exponential backoff with jitter, conditional retry predicates, maximum attempt counts, and structured logging of retry events.
A wrapper around tenacity that adds sensible production defaults and OpenTelemetry integration for retry observability.
Allow attaching logging, metrics, and header injection to every request and response without modifying call sites.
Error tracking and distributed tracing tools that capture API errors and retry sequences with full context, enabling post-incident debugging.

Error responses must not leak sensitive information. Stack traces, database query strings, internal hostnames, and secret keys must never appear in API error bodies. Sanitise exception messages before returning them.
Structured logging of errors should redact any field that may contain PII or credentials. Log the error type, status code, and request ID; do not log request bodies unless they have been sanitised.
) should be present on every response from rate-limited endpoints, not just on 429 responses. This allows clients to throttle proactively.
Classify errors before retrying: 4xx client errors should not be retried; 429 and 5xx errors should be retried with exponential backoff and jitter.
Use RFC 9457 Problem Details on the server for a standardised, machine-readable error envelope that clients can handle generically.
Exponential backoff with jitter (delay = min(cap, base * 2^n) + random) prevents thundering herd under concurrent load.
Honour Retry-After headers exactly when provided — the server knows when it will be ready.
Add idempotency keys to retried POSTs to prevent duplicate side effects on partial failures.
Set a maximum retry count and a total timeout budget to prevent infinite waits in production.
Never expose internal error details (stack traces, database queries, credentials) in API error responses.
